notes: Hamsters are small and deteriorate quickly. A hamster that has stopped eating or drinking for more than 12 hours is a true emergency. Find an exotic-experienced vet in advance - not all small animal vets see hamsters confidently.

symptoms.description: Diarrhoea, often watery, with wet matted fur around the tail and bottom. Caused by a bacterial overgrowth (Lawsonia intracellularis) usually triggered by stress. Fatal within 24-48 hours if untreated, especially in young Syrian hamsters.

possible_causes.brief: Sudden diet change or excess wet vegetables

home_care: EMERGENCY - phone an exotic vet immediately, this is life-threatening
Isolate from any other hamsters (highly contagious)
Keep warm and quiet - stress worsens the condition
Offer fresh water - dehydration is the immediate killer
Remove all fresh vegetables and fruit temporarily
Clean the cage thoroughly after vet treatment begins

vet_urgency.high: EMERGENCY - any hamster with wet bottom, watery diarrhoea, hunched or lethargic - exotic vet within hours, not the next day

red_flags: Wet, matted fur around the tail
Recently bought hamster, recently moved, or recently weaned

symptoms.description: Patches of missing fur, thinning coat, or fully bald areas. Can affect any part of the body. Causes range from harmless ageing to mites and hormone-driven disease.

possible_causes.brief: Common in older or stressed hamsters
Older hamsters thin naturally, especially around the back end
Bald patches in specific areas matching rubbing points
Circular patches, can spread to humans

home_care: Check for mites - look closely under bright light, sometimes visible as tiny moving specks
Identify any rubbing surfaces (wire bars, narrow hide entrances) and remove or modify
Improve diet variety - quality hamster pellet plus small amount of fresh food
Reduce stress and check the cage is large enough (RSPCA recommends 100cm x 50cm minimum for Syrians)
Keep skin checks frequent for spread or worsening
Wear gloves and wash hands if ringworm suspected

vet_urgency.low: Small bald patch in older hamster, no irritation, fur thinning gradually - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Spreading bald patches, possible irritation or scratching - exotic vet within 1-2 weeks
vet_urgency.high: Rapid hair loss, redness, scabs, weight loss, or multiple animals affected - urgent exotic vet

red_flags: Red, sore, or crusty skin underneath
Lumps under the skin in bald areas
Multiple hamsters in same area affected

combinations.concern: Likely mites or skin infection - vet treatment

symptoms.description: Hamster is unusually quiet, sleeping more than normal, or not running on the wheel at night. Important note: hamsters are nocturnal, so daytime sleeping is normal - look for changes from the hamster's own typical activity at night.

possible_causes.brief: Lethargy is often the first non-specific sign
Pet hamsters can enter dangerous torpor below about 16°C
Hamsters typically live 2-3 years and slow down toward the end

home_care: Check the room temperature - it should be 18-22°C (cold rooms cause hibernation-like torpor)
Warm a torpid hamster slowly with body heat or a warm room - never sudden direct heat
Check food and water are accessible and bottles are working
Weigh on a kitchen scale - hamsters should be 100-150g (Syrian) or 30-50g (dwarf)
Note any other changes (eating, drinking, droppings, posture)
Reduce stress - quieter location if possible

vet_urgency.low: Quieter than usual but still eating, drinking, moving when needed - monitor 24 hours
vet_urgency.medium: Notably less active for over 24 hours, mild appetite change - exotic vet within a couple of days
vet_urgency.high: Hunched, eyes closed, unresponsive, cold to touch, or not eating - urgent exotic vet

red_flags: Cold to the touch (possible torpor / hypothermia)
Eyes half closed during their active period

combinations.concern: Serious illness or pain - urgent care

symptoms.description: Hamster has stopped eating or eating noticeably less. Hamsters should always have food and most actively stockpile in their pouches and around the cage. A genuine appetite loss is a serious sign.

possible_causes.condition: Wet tail or other GI infection

possible_causes.brief: Newly acquired hamsters can take a few days

home_care: Check the cheek pouches gently - they should be empty when the hamster is resting
Look at the front teeth - they should be even and not too long
Offer favourite foods, small pieces of soft fruit or cucumber
Check water bottle is working (lift up and test)
Weigh daily - any loss in a hamster of this size is significant
Provide a soft food option (soaked pellets, baby food) if eating is painful

vet_urgency.low: Skipped a meal but otherwise active and exploring at night - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Eating much less for 12-24 hours - exotic vet within a day or two
vet_urgency.high: Not eating for 24+ hours, or any not eating combined with hunched or lethargic posture - urgent exotic vet

red_flags: Combined with hunched posture or lethargy
Cheek pouches look full but the hamster has not eaten

symptoms.description: Droppings are unusually soft, loose, or watery. Differs from true wet tail which is dramatic, often watery and with a strong smell, but any diarrhoea is serious in a hamster.

possible_causes.brief: Sudden change or excess wet vegetables
Some antibiotics destroy hamster gut flora

home_care: Remove all fresh vegetables and fruit immediately - return only after recovery
Provide quality plain hamster pellets and unlimited hay only
Ensure fresh water at all times - dehydration is the main risk
Keep warm and quiet to reduce stress
Clean the cage thoroughly to reduce re-infection
Phone an exotic vet immediately if any wetness around the tail or hunched posture

vet_urgency.low: Slightly soft droppings after introducing a new food, hamster otherwise well - remove new food and monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent loose droppings for over 12 hours - exotic vet within a day
vet_urgency.high: Watery diarrhoea, wet matted fur around tail, lethargy - EMERGENCY (suspected wet tail)

red_flags: Wet, matted fur around the bottom
Smell - wet tail typically has a strong odour

symptoms.description: Hamster is visibly thinner, with prominent hip bones or spine. Weight loss of even a few grams matters in such a small animal.

possible_causes.brief: Low quality food or competition with cagemate

home_care: Weigh weekly on a kitchen scale and track
Provide quality pellets plus varied small portions of suitable fresh food
Check teeth - overgrown or uneven incisors cause weight loss
Provide soft food options if eating seems painful
Separate cagemates if competition for food is suspected
Note other symptoms (thirst, urine output, appetite, energy)

vet_urgency.low: Small weight fluctuation in an otherwise healthy hamster - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Steady weight loss of 5-10% over a couple of weeks - exotic vet within a week
vet_urgency.high: Rapid loss, very prominent hips/spine, combined with other symptoms - urgent vet

red_flags: Drinking much more than normal (diabetes sign)

symptoms.description: Any new lump felt or seen on the body - under the skin, on the face, near the genitals, or in the cheek pouch area. Common in hamsters from middle age onward.

possible_causes.brief: Hot, possibly soft, sometimes leaking pus
Hamsters have high rates of tumours, especially after 1 year
Stuck food creating a hard lump near the cheek
Syrians have a scent gland on each hip - can look like a lump

home_care: Note the size, location, texture, and whether it's hot to touch
Check whether it's the scent gland (Syrians have one on each hip - small, oily, slightly raised)
Do not try to drain or squeeze - can cause infection or rupture
Photograph weekly to track growth
Keep the area clean if the lump is broken or weeping
Watch for any change in behaviour or appetite

vet_urgency.low: Small, stable, non-painful lump in known location (e.g. scent gland) - photograph monthly
vet_urgency.medium: New lump, growing slowly, hamster otherwise well - exotic vet within 1-2 weeks
vet_urgency.high: Rapidly growing, hot, ulcerated, painful, or affecting eating/movement - urgent vet

red_flags: Rapid growth (visible difference week to week)
Hamster losing weight or becoming lethargic

combinations.concern: Likely cancer or major abscess - urgent vet
If lump is on face/jaw, dental abscess possible - urgent

symptoms.description: Eye is held closed, watering, crusted shut, swollen, or bulging out. Can affect one or both eyes.

possible_causes.brief: Switch to paper-based or aspen bedding
One eye affected, often with squinting

home_care: Switch to paper or aspen bedding - never cedar or pine shavings (toxic) or dusty sawdust
Gently clean discharge with cotton wool dampened in cooled boiled water
Check for any sharp objects, cage edges, or aggressive cagemates
Do not use any human eye drops without vet guidance
Keep the cage clean and well ventilated
Take a clear photo of the eye to show the vet

vet_urgency.low: Mild watering, no other symptoms, recent bedding change - try new bedding and monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent discharge, partly closed eye, mild swelling - exotic vet within a few days
vet_urgency.high: Bulging or popped out eye, both eyes affected, eye injury, or hamster unwell - urgent vet

combinations.concern: Eye pain affecting eating - urgent vet
Possible dental abscess or tumour - urgent

symptoms.description: Hamster walks oddly, drags a leg, falls over, tilts to one side, or circles. Can affect just one limb or the whole body.

possible_causes.brief: Sudden one-sided weakness or head tilt
Hamsters can fall from owners' hands or high cages
Often from a leg caught in wire bars or wheel

home_care: Remove the wheel and any climbing furniture - prevent further injury
Place food and water at ground level within easy reach
Use a single-level cage temporarily with deep, soft bedding
Switch to solid floors if currently on wire (which can trap limbs)
Note timing - sudden onset suggests stroke or injury; gradual onset suggests arthritis or tumour
Keep warm - poorly mobile hamsters lose heat quickly

vet_urgency.low: Mild stiffness in an older hamster, no other symptoms - monitor and reduce climbing opportunities
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent limp or wobbliness - exotic vet within a few days
vet_urgency.high: Sudden onset, dragging legs, head tilt, circling, or combined with other symptoms - urgent vet

red_flags: Cannot use one or both back legs
Combined with breathing difficulty or loss of consciousness

combinations.concern: Inner ear infection or stroke - urgent vet

symptoms.description: Hamster drinks much more water than normal and produces wetter, more frequent urine patches than usual. Diabetes is particularly common in Campbell's dwarf and Chinese hamsters.

possible_causes.brief: Campbell's and Chinese are especially prone
Some commercial mixes are high in fruit/sugar

home_care: Measure water intake roughly - mark the bottle and check daily
Reduce sugary foods (commercial fruit/yoghurt drops, raisins, sweet fruit)
Provide quality plain pellets and unlimited hay
Note urination spots and frequency
Test urine glucose if diabetes is suspected (human diabetic strips work) - bring results to the vet
Weigh weekly to track changes

vet_urgency.low: Slight increase in drinking with no other symptoms - reduce sugary food and monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Sustained increased thirst and urination - exotic vet for blood and urine tests
vet_urgency.high: Combined with weight loss, lethargy, sweet-smelling urine, or sudden onset - urgent vet

combinations.concern: Likely diabetes or kidney disease - urgent vet
